summary: Julia Evans announces a new zine collaboration with Katie Sylor-Miller called 'Oh shit, Git!' that helps people fix common git mistakes. The zine was inspired by Katie's website ohshitgit.com, which explains how to recover from git mishaps. About 50% of the content is new, including fundamental explanations of how git works internally. Julia reflects on the challenge of writing about git because she had forgotten what it felt like to be confused by it, and notes this is her fifth zine of 2018.
key_points: The zine was a collaboration born from Katie Sylor-Miller's ohshitgit.com website, making it Julia's first collaborative zine project
About half the zine is new material including fundamentals about how git commits and branches work, because effective git usage requires understanding the internals
Julia struggled to write about git on her own because she'd forgotten what it was like to be confused by it, highlighting how expertise can make it harder to teach beginners
The zine format serves as a paper reference for common git recovery scenarios that feel scary to less experienced users
Julia produced five zines in 2018 and acknowledges a tradeoff between zine-writing and blogging
money_quotes: I was in this weird place with git where, even though I know that git is really confusing, I felt like I'd forgotten what it was like to be confused/scared by Git.
I write most things from a place of 'I was super confused by this thing just recently, let me explain it!!'
To really work with Git effectively you need to understand at least a little bit about how commits and branches work!
A lot of the things on that site ('oh shit, i committed to the wrong branch!') are things I remember being really scary when I was less comfortable with git!
There's a definite tradeoff between writing zines and blogging, and writing blog posts is really fun.
key_insight: Expertise can paradoxically make it harder to teach a topic because you forget what it was like to be confused, and collaboration with someone who remembers that confusion can bridge the gap.
